Q1.label: Question 1
Q1.scenario: Country X has been experiencing higher than usual inflation over the past year. It is expected that this period of higher inflation will last for at least another 5 to 10 years.

(i).question: Discuss how higher than expected inflation over the next 5 years could impact a health and care insurance company’s business in general, other than claim rates and claim amounts.
(i).synopsis: Inflation Risk, Risk Mitigation, Premium Re-pricing

Solution: Extent of risk management utilized at Company Y If risk mitigation techniques are employed against inflation exposure, the impact could be minimal. [½]
The extent of this will depend on the duration of any risk mitigation techniques implemented. [½]
There may be a risk of increased costs to mitigate inflation risk e.g. if the duration is 3 years, it may be more expensive to mitigate risks for the next 2 years. [½]
Sources of risk Premium Rates For group business, premiums are adjusted annually and as such there is less exposure to inflation risk than individual business. [½]
For individual business, products that are renewed annually will be re-priced on renewal and as such there is less exposure to inflation risk than individual longer term business. [½]
e.g. individual PMI, Health Cash Plans renewed annually can be re-priced for inflation whereas 10, 15 year Critical Illness or Income Protection products will not be. [½]
Although regular material re-pricing on shorter term products may affect lapse rates as policyholders may not be happy with significant annual premium increases. [½]
Commission Commission payments - if linked to premiums, these may increase as premiums may increase to reflect inflation. [½]

Commission payments - if linked to sum insured, distributors may demand an increase to reflect a need for higher income in compensation for bringing in business - this would not be as immediate as if commission is linked to premiums. [½]
Investment Investment returns may be eroded in value. [½]
Market reaction to high inflation environment could impact the attractiveness of different asset classes. [½]
These issues could put pressure on the company to change its investment strategy unless [½]
Inflation swaps are in place to limit extreme inflation exposure risk [½]
Or the company already has a good asset matching relative to liabilities to reduce exposure to inflation. [½]
Accessing some matching assets such as inflation linked bonds may be difficult if the market becomes less liquid [½]
Or the price of these assets may increase significantly due to increased demand. [½]
Competition Consumers are more price sensitive in a high inflation environment so the insurance market may become more competitive on price. [½]
If the insurer can afford to keep premiums lower, while other insurers adjust their premiums, this may allow new sales to be attracted and for the insurer to capture the market. [½]
If others in the market can ‘weather the storm’ of inflation in the short term better than the insurer, then the insurer may be at a disadvantage in terms of new sales and persistency. [½]
This may have a knock-on effect on profits as existing expense loadings may not be adequate when split over a lower than expected business volume. [½]
If this churning is anti-selective, it may leave a worse pool of experience i.e. those that will not choose to go through underwriting again. [½]
Persistency Lapses may have a mixed affect, potentially based on socio-economic status or sum insured. [½]
Policyholders may have less money to maintain their insurance policies and may therefore need to lapse their policies. [½]
Alternatively, policy holders may see the value in their insurance in uncertain times and be less willing to lapse their policies. [½]
Some policyholders might choose to reduce the sum insured on their policy in order to reduce the premium on their policy to an affordable level. [½]
Lapses might be selective; [½]
Those policyholders who are healthiest may take the risk of not having insurance and so may be willing to lapse [½]
Those individuals who are less healthy, who would be unlikely to get through underwriting again un-rated, will be less likely to lapse. [½]
Retention may be higher for group business as companies try to retain employees with benefits in kind rather than salary increases. [½]
Possible increased risk of churning by distributors, to obtain more sales commission if new business sales are low. [½]
Options & Guarantees Some options may be more likely to ‘come into the money’ in a higher inflation market. [½]
This might reduce the profitability of the option overall. [½]
This environment may make guarantees more appealing to the consumer. [½]
E.g. guaranteed premium rates. [½]
New Business (volume, mix) New business sales may be lower for individual business [½]
e.g. when budgets are tight, funds for insurance may be limited. [½]
The business mix may alter to being a more affluent group who can still afford premiums when other living costs are increasing. [½]
The company’s cost of attracting new business may be higher than expected where sales, distribution and marketing costs could have risen significantly due to inflation [½]
Making it more expensive for the company to expand its new business. [½]
Expenses Higher inflation will lead to increased costs of the insurer running its business. [½]
Higher inflation could increase: Company’s fixed expenses. For example: [½]
Office rents [½]
IT equipment [½]
Company’s variable expenses may also be higher [½]
E.g. higher inflation could lead to higher staff salaries. [½]
Which could put pressure on the company to increase premium rates. [½]
Reinsurance Reinsurance premiums may not be affected in the short-term if the reinsurance treaty terms have been recently agreed. [½]
Reinsurance premiums may increase to reflect the increase in the reinsurer’s general costs. [½]
Reinsurance premiums could increase for the product lines most exposed to inflation risk by the reinsurer. [½]
Dependent on how the economic environment is affecting the reinsurer. [½]
E.g the reinsurer may have a lower exposure to Country X’s high inflation and so be less concerned about general costs increasing. [½]
Actions of management Management may be inclined to take action or change the strategy if the increased inflation is affecting the company’s share price. [½]
Non-disclosure In a bid to reduce premiums, consumers may be less inclined to provide full disclosure at the time of underwriting [½]
So increased risk of fraud [½]
And possibly higher costs for the company to investigate and resolve claims. [½]
Operational risk New and/or replacement hiring of employees may be reduced to manage expenses, thereby increasing the work burden on existing employees which may increase turnover [½]
Which could increase the risk of operational errors occurring. [½]
Counterparty risk If the reinsurer is downgraded, this could increase counterparty risk. [½]
Other parties may be struggling in a higher inflation market to meet outgoings; [½]
The insurer may find some outsourced contracts are ceased or fees increased [½]
Or the quality of work reduces below contract standards, if personnel change or investment in operations is halted. [½]
State benefits If state benefits do not keep up with inflation rates, then private insurance may seem more appealing to a greater number of people in society [½]
Which could increase sales in private health and care insurance. [½]

(ii).question:
Company Y is a large health and care insurance company operating in Country X.
Company Y sells income protection, critical illness, private medical insurance and hospital cash plans on an individual and group basis.
Describe how higher than expected inflation over the next 5 years may impact the claim rates and claim amounts for each of the product lines sold by Company Y:
(a) income protection
(b) critical illness
(c) private medical insurance
(d) hospital cash plans.
(ii).synopsis: Economic Downturn, claims Experience, Hospital Cash Plans

Solution: Income Protection
Critical Illness
Private Medical Insurance
Hospital Cash Plans [maximu m 3 marks] Claim rate Economic downturn and/or jobs at risk may mean people more likely to bring forward a claim. [½]
However, HCP are intended to keep premiums lower so there may be a no-claims discount or excess limit which deters an increase in claim rates. [½]

Less likely to have higher rate of claims in the short term as hospital cash plans are usually for larger surgeries or longer hospital stays which will be linked to the health of the individual (and not inflation). [½]

However, over the medium to longer term this may be impacted by the ability of the health care system to meet the public’s needs with increasing costs and the ability of the individual to meet smaller out of pocket expenses to ‘stay healthy’. [½]

Those no longer able to afford PMI or who are unhealthier lives may choose HCP. Or those who are healthy and lapse out of PMI to save money may choose to take HCP in lieu. The magnitude of this change in business mix will determine if the claim rate goes up (for the former) or down (for the latter). [½]
Claim amount Benefits are fixed [½]
So no change in the short term [½]
Although the real value of the benefits paid to policyholders will be less. [½]
It becomes more likely that over time the current benefit levels are no longer suitable. [½]
This puts new business sales and renewal at risk. [½]
Therefore the claim amounts may increase as the benefit amounts are increased to reflect price increases long term. [½]
[Overall maximum of 10 marks]

(iii).question:
The board of Company Y has expressed concerns about the impacts of higher than expected inflation on the profitability of its product lines and has asked what actions could be taken to mitigate these impacts.
Discuss the possible actions Company Y could take to mitigate the impacts of higher than expected inflation on the profitability of its product lines.
(iii).synopsis: Inflation Strategy, Business Mix, Reviewable Premiums

Solution: General Actions Extent of risk management utilized at Company Y Review Company Y’s existing risk mitigation techniques employed against inflation exposure [½]
And adjust existing risk management techniques where appropriate. [½]
New business Ensure new business premium rates for both individual and group business are updated to allow for latest inflation expectations. [½]
Consider changing the mix of business written by Company Y to reduce the impact of higher inflation on profitability. [½]
Consider writing more business with reviewable premiums [½]
As these are less exposed to risk than business with guaranteed premiums. [½]
For example: Consider writing more group business than individual business, as group premiums are adjusted annually and as such are less exposed to higher inflation risk. [½]
Consider writing more short-term business than longer-term business. [½]
Whether Company Y decides to make major changes to the business mix it sells will depend on how long the period of high inflation is expected to occur. [½]
Company Y may decide not to make major changes to its business mix if the period of high inflation was expected to be short. [½]
Whatever actions are taken Company Y will need to closely monitor business mix in the future and assess the profitability impact of any change in business mix. [½]
Competition Carry out a review of the actions that competitors are taking in the market. [½]
Company Y will need to carefully consider the impact of its actions on its own competitive position. [½]

For example, if Company Y’s competitors in the market decide to not make major changes in the short term to premium rates etc then Company Y could be at a disadvantage in terms of new sales and persistency if it were to significantly increase premium rates or change its terms and conditions [½]
Which could adversely impacts its profits. [½]

Company Y could find that adverse changes to its persistency because of a worsening competitive position could be anti-selective for its existing business, and could leave Company Y with a worse pool of experience. [½]
Investment mismatching Review the extent of risk exposure in terms of how well matched the claims and expenses outflow is by asset-matching. [½]
Consider putting in place inflation swaps to limit extreme inflation exposure risk [½]
But need to consider cost of putting in place inflation swaps in current high inflation environment. [½]
Consider improving asset matching to strategy to reduce exposure to inflation. [½]
However matching assets such as inflation linked bonds may be difficult or expensive to buy if the market becomes less liquid or the price much higher. [½]
This may restrict ability to improve asset matching strategy. [½]
Company Y could prefer selling more short-term products where it is potentially much easier to match cashflow outgoes by adjusting premiums [½]
And the risk exposure on longer-term products is harder to match on those products where the benefit value is unknown, as well as the timing. [½]
Policyholder Actions Policyholders may have less funds for insurance and so lapse. The company may wish to employ a retention team to reduce the number of lapses. [½]
The impact on lapses rates may vary by socio-economic status or sum insured. The company could provide more incentive to those policies covering a larger expense loading. [½]

However, policyholders may also see the value of their insurance more during uncertain times and so be more likely to renew. Given the impact may be unclear, the company should review lapse experience by business mix criteria to understand any changes. [½]
The company may wish to provide optionality to policyholders to improve renewals and retention. [½]
This may include allowing increases in the sum insured as it is being reduced in real terms by inflation. [½]
Alternatively, allow policyholders to reduce the sum insureds in order to reduce the premium to an affordable level. [½]

As lapses may be selective; those policyholders who are healthiest may take the risk of not having insurance vs. those who are unlikely to get through underwriting again un-rated who do not lapse. The company may wish to review the appropriateness of its pricing basis in case it included cross subsidies. [½]

Retention may be higher for group busness as companies try to retain employees with benefits in kind rather than salary increases. The company may wish to reassess its sales strategy to focus more on group business, if sales are easier attain. [½]
There may be a risk of churning by distributors; to create sales commission if new business sales are low. The company may wish to consider implementing commission claw back terms. [½]
Options & Guarantees Company Y will need consider the impact of changing any options or guarantees on new business. [½]
Removing any options or guarantees on new business that were likely to bite in higher inflation environment could lead to improving profits. [½]
However removing or reducing the options/guarantees could make Company Y’s products less appealing to the consumer [½]
Which could result in a fall in new business. [½]
Expenses Consider options to mitigate the impacts of higher expenses on fixed expenses [½]
E.g. If there are forthcoming rent reviews then need to negotiate new office rents. [½]
Could look for less expensive office space. [½]
Could negotiate to limit salary increases [½]
But staff likely to be suffering impacts of higher than expected inflation [½]
And poor salary increases could lead to a loss of key staff. [½]
Reinsurance Company should review the impact of higher inflation on its reinsurance terms. [½]
Company Y could look to renegotiate reinsurance terms if treaties are coming up for renewal [½]
And the cost of reinsurance has increased significantly [½]
Or Company Y could look to find more acceptable terms with another reinsurer. [½]
Non-disclosure Company Y could look to tighten its underwriting processes to try to identify consumers not providing full disclosure at the time of underwriting. [½]
Commission Outgo Review impact of higher inflation on commission outgo. [½]
Where commission rates are impacted by high inflation could look to reduce them [½]
But this could adversely impact future sales through intermediaries if competitors do not decrease their commission rates. [½]
Product Specific Actions Income Protection Company Y may wish to consider reducing new individual IP business [½]
As the IP benefit is linked to earnings outside of claim [½]
And the policy can be in payment multiple times over the duration of cover. [½]
Therefore expected outgo for IP has greater scope to materially change due to impacts of higher inflation than some of the other products Company Y sells. [½]

Also Company Y may prefer to sell less IP which has unknown benefit amounts, with unknown timing and duration which could be harder to find assets to provide a suitable match for claim payments in periods of high inflation. [½]

Critical Illness Company Y could potentially look to write more CI business in a high inflation environment as CI benefit is usually a fixed sum insured and is paid once to terminate the contract. [½]
Hence, the expected CI claims outgo is therefore not materially impacted by claims inflation. [½]

However, CI has potentially labour-intensive claims review processes which may involve several tiers of internal review by staff and potentially medics so increasing the labour intensity and labour costs will increase in a high inflation environment. [½]
Also CI business is medically underwritten on an individual basis which can be somewhat labour intensive and therefore underwriting costs are likely to be impacted by higher inflation. [½]
Private Medical Insurance Company Y could consider writing more PMI [½]
As PMI premiums are usually reset each year and as such have lower risk exposure to inflation than long term products like CI and IP. [½]
PMI is often sold with a moratorium period. Therefore this product is less exposed to higher expenses inflation than products with relatively higher underwriting. [½]

For PMI, the claim is based on a treatment occurring so underwriting at time of claim is limited to non-disclosure and so claim expenses are less exposed to inflation than products with more subjective claim stage underwriting. [½]
Introduce claims excess to reduce claim amounts and possibly claim inception rates. [½]
Hospital cash plans Company Y could consider writing more HCP’s as they are potentially less exposed to inflation than some of the other products Company Y sells because: [½]
HCP premiums are usually reset each year and as such have lower risk exposure to higher inflation than longer term products like CI and IP. [½]
HCPs are designed to be lower underwriting at the time of sale so underwriting costs should not be particularly adversely impacted by higher inflation. [½]

HCPs are not labour intensive to underwrite claims as these are based on treatment and so should be a straightforward underwriting at time of claim of proof of treatment so claims costs should not be particularly adversely impacted by high inflation. [½]

(iv).question:
Due to the expectations about future inflation, Company Y is reassessing the loadings applied to the underlying risk premium to reach the final office premiums for new business.
Suggest possible investigations Company Y could carry out to test how sensitive future new business volumes for its products could be to a change in final office premium rates.
(iv).synopsis: Customer Research, Market Surveys, Distribution Channels

Solution: Market research via: [½]
An online consumer survey [½]
A focus group [½]
Interview distributors [½]
Run a test sales strategy with phases by price [½]
Online [½]
Via comparison website [½]
In specific regions of Country X [½]
Work with industry bodies [½]
Comparison websites to understand data on sales and price for similar products [½]
Work with internal stakeholders [½]
The retention team may have data on what price adjustment ‘retains’ a policy [½]
The sales team may have data on historic price adjustments and the impact on business volumes [½]
Ask consultancies for previous research [½]
Ask reinsurers for previous work/research [½]
Review academic or industry previous work [½]
Company could analyse its own historic data to see how new business volumes were impacted by changes in premium rates [½]
[Reasonable suggestions, not listed above, can be awarded ½ mark each]

(v).question:
Initial results from the investigations suggest that the final office premium for both critical illness and income protection could be increased, without reducing sales volumes, for the higher socio-economic business mix segments.
Expenses are currently loaded as a fixed amount per policy across all products and business segments. The marketing actuary has suggested charging the highest final office premium, as indicated by the investigations, by adjusting how expenses are loaded.
Describe which ways of loading expenses to the underlying risk premium may better meet this objective.
(v).synopsis: Expense Allocation, Socio-economic Targeting, loading methods

Solution: Expenses can be loaded with regard to the premium, the sum insured, a fixed amount per policy, or a combination of all of the above. [Half mark for 2 examples, 1 mark for 3 or more] The target is to only alter the loading for CI and IP and so different approaches should be adopted by product line. [½]
The fixed amount per policy could be specific to product (IP and CI) but would not be specific to the higher socio-economic grouping target. [½]
Expense loading by premium might unfairly allocate expenses to those in lower socio-economic groups who have lower sum insureds but higher premiums due to rating at time of underwriting. [½]
Expense loading by sum insured would best meet this objective as it is most related to higher socio-economic group for CI and IP. [½]

(vi).question:
–3 The pricing actuary has suggested that other adjustments to the underlying risk premium are likely given the high inflation environment. It has therefore been suggested that any change to the final office premiums be postponed until a full review of all adjustments is undertaken.
The pricing actuary anticipates that this full review will cause a reassessment in the sales strategy by product line.
Explain why a full review may cause a reassessment of the sales strategy by product line.
(vi).synopsis: Premium Adjustments, Profit Margins, Competitive Strategy

Solution: The calculated premium is made up of the underlying risk premium and many adjustments including: adjusted for expenses adjusted for profit margin + adjusted for commission + adjusted for cost of capital + adjusted for cost of reserves + adjusted for reinsurance cost and recoveries + adjusted for margins + adjusted for investment returns + adjustment for persistency + adjusted for tax [½]
Therefore, there are many adjustments, other than the expense loading, which may be altered for higher inflation or the alteration to the sources of risk (see section (i)). [½]
These adjustments may increase for some product lines more than others depending on the inflation risk exposure. [½]
These adjustments may increase for some business mix segments more than others depending on the inflation risk exposure. [½]
Due to the high inflation environment the insurer may seek higher margins on products with a greater exposure to unmitigated inflation risk, such as individual IP. [½]
The calculated premium therefore must then be considered in terms of the business strategy for the insurer [½]
E.g. what is the insurer’s risk appetite on each product (i.e. to grow, reduce, maintain). [½]
The insurer must considers this in relation to the competitive environment it operates within [½]
E.g. how other insurers are responding to the market conditions [½]
E.g. how distributors are responding to insurers’ price changes and commission [½]
E.g. how consumers are responding to price changes. [½]
The company must strategically consider if it is able to win the required/desired business volume at that calculated premium level. [½]
The company may need to assess realistic estimates of the expected levels of new business across each product line. [½]
The calculated premium, after accounting for the increased inflation risk, may not match with the price elasticity in the market as shown by the investigations. [½]
The insurer could make adjustments to that calculated premium to arrive at the final market premium which reflects the insurers sales strategy by product line. [½]

The final office premium; which is a sum of the calculated premium + adjusted for market conditions (commercial pricing with reference to competitors) + position in the underwriting cycle + ability to add profit margin + premium frequency + distribution channels + loss leaders, discounts, or cross subsidies + any regulatory minimum or maximum premium levels. [½]
At this final call in the sales strategy, if the company cannot achieve acceptable levels of profit for certain product lines it could: [½]
Sell these product lines as a loss leader [½]
Or stop selling these product lines [½]
Or look to substitute sales of the product line with more profitable product lines. [½]

Q2.label: Question 2
Q2.scenario:
XYZ Health is a health and care insurance company that sells Critical Illness (CI)
business in Country A.
XYZ Health’s CI product covers a number of different health conditions, but currently it does not cover any mental health conditions.
XYZ Health has received many requests to add mental health conditions to its CI product. Financial advisors and policyholders have argued for many years that individuals in Country A can be subject to serious mental health conditions during their lifetime and the impact of this could be as debilitating as other major CIs.
Therefore, it is appropriate for serious mental health conditions to be covered by XYZ Health’s CI product.

(i).question: Comment on the suitability of covering serious mental health conditions in an individual CI product.
(i).synopsis: CI Product Design, Mental Health severe impact, prevalence

Solution: The conditions covered by a critical illness insurance product tend to be those that: are perceived as severe [½]
that can occur frequently [½]
that can be defined clearly so that there is no ambiguity at time of claim [½]
and for which there is sufficient data available to price the benefit. [½]
Some mental health conditions have similar features. [1]
For example: Some mental health conditions could have as debilitating an impact on the life of an individual as many health related critical illnesses [½]
Therefore the impact on the individual could be severe. [½]
Many individuals could have a mental health condition during their life [½]
This means claims from mental health conditions could occur frequently. [½]
Provided the mental health conditions can be well defined and measurable then there may be no ambiguity at the time of claim [½]
But it may not be possible to define some mental health conditions and it may not be suitable to include those conditions. [½]
XYZ will initially not have any data to price the risk from mental health conditions [½]
However if the frequency of claims is significant an insurer may be able to build up sufficient data to price the risk from mental health conditions quickly. [½]

XYZ will need to carefully consider what underwriting requirements it will have with respect to mental health conditions as these could be quite different to XYZ’s existing underwriting requirements. [½]
Therefore some mental health conditions are potentially suitable to be covered by a critical illness product. [½]
Adding mental health conditions could differentiate XYZ’s product from its competitors [½]
And make XYZ look innovative. [½]
[Any sensible comments up to 1 mark]

(ii).question:
The sales director of XYZ Health has proposed that a small number of serious mental health conditions are added to the conditions covered by XYZ Health’s individual CI product for new business only.
Discuss if the sales director’s proposal is likely to meet the needs of CI policyholders.
(ii).synopsis: Policyholder Needs, CI Definitions, Customer Satisfaction

Solution: Arguments for meetings the needs of policyholders Whether it meets the needs of CI policyholders (or not) will depend on the mental health conditions that XYZ adds [½]
And how these conditions are defined. [½]
If the mental health conditions added have a high level of prevalence in the general population of Country A adding those mental health conditions could be attractive to many policyholders. [½]
Particularly those policyholders who may be at risk of the mental health conditions that are to be added. [½]
Or already have existing mental health conditions. [½]
A number of existing policyholders have asked for XYZ to add mental health conditions so adding mental health conditions will potentially meet these policyholder’s concerns. [½]
Arguments against meeting the needs of policyholders: The new mental health conditions will only be in respect of new business and will not impact existing policyholders [½]
Existing policyholders are unlikely to be happy that they will not be covered for mental health conditions. [½]
Adding a small number of pre-specified mental health conditions will not help new policyholders who have a mental health condition that is not on the list of pre-specified conditions. [½]
Policyholders could be disappointed if certain mental health conditions are not on the list. [½]
Adding mental health conditions is likely to increase the premium paid by policyholders. [½]
Depending on the mental conditions added the increase in cost could be significant. [½]
And not all critical illness policyholders will want that. [½]
Many policyholders may prefer a cheaper critical illness policy covering what they perceive as more traditional critical illness conditions (heart attack, stroke etc). [1]
Potential policyholders may not be happy answering additional underwriting questions about their mental health. [½]
Depending on the mental health conditions added there may be difficulties in validating claims. [½]
[Any additional relevant points up to 1 mark]

(iii).question: Discuss the factors XYZ Health should consider when determining the serious mental health conditions to be covered in its CI product.
(iii).synopsis: Mental Health Claim Triggers, Selective Coverage, common conditions

Solution: There are many possible types of mental health conditions with varying degrees of severity for the individual. [½]
So XYZ will need to be selective about the mental health conditions that it decides to cover. [½]
It may be that a small number of mental health conditions will cover most types of likely claim from policyholders in the country. [½]
XYZ should look to obtain data about what the most common mental health conditions are in the country. [½]
Similarly, to health conditions covered by a CI policy XYZ is likely want to cover mental health conditions that seriously impact a policyholder’s life. [1]
For example: Where the policyholder has not been able to work for a period (e.g. 12 months). [½]
Where the policyholder has been hospitalised for a period of x weeks. [½]

It is possible that some mental health conditions could be temporary or curable and hence paying out on a critical illness claim for one of these conditions could result in a windfall payment for policyholders. [½]
It is not likely to be appropriate to include these mental health conditions to XYZ Health’s critical illness product. [½]
To be included as a mental health condition XYZ Health will need to be able to define the mental health conditions accurately. [½]
XYZ may need specialist medical advice when defining potentially complex mental health conditions in its terms and conditions. [½]
XYZ could work with specialists such as reinsurers with respect to claim definitions [½]
Reinsurers could also provide information about what mental health conditions they will cover and the potential costs of getting reinsurance cover. [½]
Level of underwriting could be too intrusive for certain mental health conditions. [½]
Review similar products in other countries to assess what mental health conditions have been added there. [½]
XYZ will need to consider if it will be willing to make partial benefit payments on mental health conditions for lower levels of impairment, whilst the remaining cover stays in place. [½]
XYZ will need to consider the particular mental health conditions covered by competitors, and whether it will cover similar conditions or different mental health conditions. [½]
Offering coverage of different mental health conditions could give XYZ Health a competitive advantage. [½]
There may be industry standard wording describing mental health conditions (perhaps put together by a regulator or insurance industry body) that may have been developed to avoid this. [½]
[Any additional relevant points up to 1 mark]

(iv).question: Discuss the impact of the sales director’s proposal on the competitive position of XYZ Health’s CI product.
(iv).synopsis: Pricing competitiveness, consumer attractiveness, premium impact

Solution: Any increase in premium rates could reduce the price competitiveness of XYZ Health’s product [1]
And hence could reduce future new business. [½]
However, the addition of mental health benefits could make XYZ’s product more attractive to consumers [1]
And this could compensate for the price rise and increase new business. [½]
The overall impact on new business is likely to depend on the size of any premium increase [½]
And whether policyholders feel the mental health conditions added are conditions are appropriate. [½]
The impact on new business will also depend on what other competitors already do. [½]
If other competitors do not cover mental health conditions, then XYZ could have a CI product that is more attractive to consumers [½]
In particular as the company will have a first mover advantage. [½]
Leading to an increase in new business. [½]
Although any increase in new business sales may be temporary if competitors follow XYZ’s example. [½]
If competitors already cover mental health conditions then the company may need to include them in order to maintain its overall competitiveness. [½]

(v).question: Describe any changes XYZ Health may need to make to its underwriting approach if it adds serious mental health conditions to its CI product.
(v).synopsis: Mental Health Underwriting, Pre-existing conditions, risk loadings

Solution: Underwriting is used to adjust the insurance premium that a policyholder pays for factors that have a bearing on the individual’s risk. [½]

XYZ will want to ensure that the underwriting process identifies individuals with mental health conditions who have higher risk of claiming than assumed within its standard critical illness pricing. [1]
XYZ will need to decide what level of loadings is appropriate to reflect increased risk for an individual. [½]
XYZ may decide that individuals with minor existing mental health issues can still be written on standard terms. [½]
XYZ is likely to want to use its standard underwriting procedures for determining whether an individual with mental health conditions has a higher level of risk than standard [½]
Rather than try to come up with completely new underwriting procedures for mental health conditions alone. [½]
The insurer will need to consider pre-existing mental health conditions. [1]
It is likely that XYZ Health is likely to want an individual to complete some additional underwriting questions in respect of their mental health. [½]
An example of this could be that XYZ will want to know about pre-existing mental health conditions where this is likely to have a material impact on the level of risk for an individual. [½]
XYZ may need specialist medical advice when developing the underwriting questions that policyholders will need to answer. [½]

As is the case with asking questions about health conditions XYZ will want to ensure that any questions about an individual’s mental health are asked clearly, are relevant to an individual’s mental health [½]
And are proportionate and asked sensitively. [½]
XYZ will wish to avoid underwriting questions about mental health that cannot be answered without medical expertise or understanding. [½]
XYZ will want to ensure that all questions comply with relevant regulation and laws in Country A. [½]
XYZ will look to be as transparent as possible about underwriting decisions and pricing aspects of mental health. [½]
As part of the underwriting process medical specialists may need to review cases that meet specific criteria. [½]

XYZ will want to ensure that its underwriters continually develop and refine underwriting for mental health conditions to take into account the latest and most relevant medical evidence available. [½]

(vi).question:
XYZ Health specifically lists all the conditions that are covered under its CI product in its terms and conditions.
XYZ Health has received complaints from its CI policyholders that they cannot predict the conditions that they will contract during their lifetime, and therefore a CI product that only covers a defined list of conditions that are fixed throughout the lifetime of their policy is not flexible enough for their needs.
An example of the problem is that during a recent pandemic, many policyholders of XYZ Health contracted a serious new infectious disease that resulted in a significant level of long-term impairment for some policyholders. The new disease was not listed in XYZ Health’s CI terms and conditions and therefore XYZ Health did not pay out on any claims for this disease.
The sales director of XYZ Health has proposed that XYZ Health replace the list of individual specified infectious diseases covered under its current CI product with a catch all term of ‘serious infectious diseases’.
Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of the sales director’s proposal from the perspective of XYZ Health.
(vi).synopsis: Certainty of cover, Simplicity, Capital Requirements

Solution: Advantages of the sales director’s proposal The sales directors proposal will give the customer and the financial advisor more certainty that their CI policy will pay out in the event of the policyholder catching a disease. [1]
The proposed approach is simpler for the customer to understand and the financial advisor to explain what is covered by the policy. [½]
This will give the policyholder more confidence to buy the product in the first place. [½]
This increased certainty could make XYZ’s CI product more competitive in the market [½]
Which could lead to increased sales and profitability for XYZ. [½]
The proposal would make XYZ seem more consumer friendly [½]
And will potentially enhance XYZ’s reputation. [½]
Disadvantages of the Sales Directors proposal However, if the amended product sales are materially increased then XYZ will need to find more capital than expected. [½]
If XYZ’s capital is limited this could become an issue. [½]
It could even result in new business being stopped partway through the year. This could result in significant reputational damage. [½]
The claims definition is key to designing a sustainable and profitable product. [½]
Accurate policy wording is likely to be crucial in terms of understanding what diseases are actually covered by XYZ’s policy [½]
And therefore understanding what XYZ’s future claim experience for its CI policy is likely to be. [½]
Using a catch all term like ‘serious infectious diseases’ is potentially not an accurate term and will potentially cover a very wide range of diseases [½]
Including those that are currently known, and any other diseases that are currently not known but will emerge in the future. [½]
This will make it extremely uncertain for XYZ in terms of what diseases are actually covered by their CI product [½]
And also make estimation of future claims experience very difficult. [½]
There may end up being disputes about what constitutes a ‘serious’ infectious disease. This could lead to legal challenge. [½]
Potentially XYZ could end up paying out on claims it did not expect to pay out on. [½]
If XYZ is too prudent in assuming every possible infectious disease is covered this could make the premium for the product unaffordable for the customer. [1]
Depending on the approach taken by other health and care insurers if XYZ is too prudent in its assumptions there is risk that their CI product becomes uncompetitive. [1]
If the pricing actuary is less prudent then there is a risk that claims experience could be significantly worse than expected [½]
Which could have an adverse impact on XYZ’s profitability [½]
Over the long term it could adversely impact XYZ’s solvency. [½]
It is unlikely that past data will be much use for assessing future claims experience if XYZ moves to the new ‘catch all’ definition. [½]
There may be higher than expected claims expenses if the number of claims payouts increases [½]
Which could also reduce product profitability. [½]
The new product may need to have significantly higher claims reserves than the current product [½]
Due to higher prudential margins to reflect greater uncertainty about future experience [½]
This could adversely impact product profitability. [½]
Similarly more capital is likely to be needed to write the same level of new business. [½]
XYZ may also be faced with additional legal costs if it ends up in dispute with customers about whether claims are covered. [½]
Overall the proposal potentially creates significant uncertainty for XYZ and is not likely to be suitable. [1]
[Up to 1 mark for any conclusion on suitability that follows from the points made in the candidate’s answer to this part question.]

Q3.label: Question 3
Q3.scenario: Company X, a health and care insurance company, writes both group and individual Income Protection (IP) policies. Company X has recently analysed the claim experience of its IP business. Company X has noticed that the loss ratio (defined as claims incurred as a percentage of premium) for this business has increased steadily over the analysis period and is now substantially higher than the loss ratios reported by its competitors for IP business.

(i).question: Discuss, with reasons, what investigations you could undertake to understand the reasons for the increase in the loss ratio of Company X’s IP business.
(i).synopsis: Loss Ratio Analysis, Recovery Rates, Business Mix

Solution: The loss ratio may be higher than expected due to: Higher claims inception rates [½]
Lower recovery rates [½]
Higher claim amounts [½]
Lower premium income [½]
Investigate whether there have been changes in the business mix such as: [½]
There may be cross subsidies in the pricing basis [½]
Demographic trends (age, gender, smoker) [½]
Or by occupation [½]
And scheme size for group business to see if free cover limits are inappropriate [½]
Review whether there have been changes in product terms and conditions [½]
Or whether there have been changes in market norms [½]
For example: Incapacity definitions [½]
The deferred period [½]
Benefit limits / maximums [½]
Replacement ratios [½]
Claim criteria [½]
Underwriting criteria [½]
Inclusion of partial, proportional or rehabilitation benefits [½]
[Up to 3 marks in total for suitable examples.] Investigate claim escalation rates. [½]
A drive to increase market share with pricing discounts may have reduced premium income. [½]
Investigate whether there has been any significant changes in new business such as a major decline in new business [½]
With the average age (and related risk) of the book of the business increasing as a result [½]
Or lower protection from waiting periods [½]
Or a loss of large groups schemes with favourable experience [½]
Or other marketing campaigns which may have attracted poor risks. [½]
Review general economic conditions [½]
As recession conditions may lead to increases in claims or lapses [½]
And impact recovery rates. [½]
Economic conditions, such as changes in interest rates, may impact the value of claims in payment reserves. [½]
Conduct a lapse experience. [½]
There may be selective lapses, for example if the product is no longer competitive. [½]
Investigate cause of claims as this may impact claim duration. [½]
Investigate early claims or claims by notification date to check for anti-selective behaviour. [½]
Investigate if there have been any outliers /abnormal claims which are influencing the loss ratio. [½]
Have there been any ex-gratia claims (or other legal challenges/fines) which may impact the results [½]
Or whether there are any large group schemes which have an outsize impact on the loss ratio. [½]
Investigate whether there has been a change in the type of claim being incurred, for example, mental health or stress related claims may have lower recovery rates. [½]

(ii).question: Suggest possible actions Company X could take to improve the loss ratio.
(ii).synopsis: Pricing Remediation, product Features, replacement ratios

Solution: Could increase premium rates [½]
Or implement more granular premium rates (if not in place). [½]
Could amend product terms for example: [½]
Reduce maximum replacement ratio [½]
To encourage return to work [½]
Increase waiting periods [½]
Increase deferred periods [½]
Introduce partial or rehabilitation benefits [½]
Amend underwriting guidelines for example: [½]
Limit high risk occupations [½]
Increase health underwriting [½]
Reduce free cover limits on group schemes [½]
Review disability definitions [½]
And ensure these are clear to limit any disputes [½]
Increase claim underwriting, for example, by requiring additional documentation or second opinions [½]
Tighten claims assessment practices [½]
And claims management to ensure benefits paid and recovery times are as expected. [½]
Take measures to reduce lapses [½]
For example, introduce loyalty benefits. [½]
Introduce rehabilitation services to get customers back to work sooner. [½]
Improve fraud detection processes. [½]
Review and tighten policy wording. [½]
Get advice from reinsurer. [½]
[Any additional reasonable points up to 1 mark.]

(iii).question:
Following the investigation, Company X has concluded that a key reason for the difference in the loss ratio for IP business between Company X and its competitors is that Company X does not have a team dedicated to actively managing the treatment of IP claimants. Company X is considering setting up a team to actively manage the treatment provided for IP claimants.
Discuss the factors that Company X should consider when estimating:
(a) the costs involved in setting up this team.
(b) the benefits of setting up this team.
(iii).synopsis: Claims Management Team, Cost-benefit Analysis, skill requirements

Solution: Estimating costs in setting up this team: There will be fixed and variable costs. [½]
The cost is likely to be related to the number of people needed to manage claims. [½]
To understand the number of people required to manage claims, need to understand: Current number of claims [½]
And future expected number of claims [½]
And number of claims where claim management/intervention would improve outcomes. [½]
This is likely to involve understanding the duration of claims [½]
And the clause of claims. [½]
The cause of claims will also be important to understand the mix of skills needed [½]
Which will also impact the cost as may need to understand the salaries of different medical health professionals [½]
And expected salary inflation. [½]
Consider any other staff related costs - e.g. bonuses, social security contributions,, Pension costs. [½]
Treatment costs will also need to be estimated. [½]
There may be additional systems required to manage treatments [½]
And evaluate treatment versus ongoing claim payment [½]
Which will incur development and ongoing costs. [½]
Evaluate additional support costs - offices/ computers/furniture etc. [½]
Consider any external costs - e.g. specialist opinions for complex cases. [½]
Estimating benefits: Understand the number of claims in payment with sufficiently large reserves [½]
And the causes of these claims. [½]
There may be a once-off benefit if there are current large claim reserves for claims in payment and these have potential for return to work. [½]
There will be long term benefits from arresting the trend of increasing loss ratios and potentially reverting to the industry norm. [½]
Industry claim ratios may provide a guide to the expected benefit [½]
And data from reinsurers and/or consultants may also assist. [½]
May need to assess policy terms and conditions to understand whether treatment recommendations can be applied to existing business. [½]
May need to assess current availability of partial, proportional or rehabilitation benefits as this will impact ultimate benefit realised. [½]
There may be benefits related to brand, customer perceptions which are likely to be difficult to quantify [½]
Or increases in new business if this is seen as a value added service. [½]

(iv).question: Outline the possible risks for Company X of setting up such a team.
(iv).synopsis: Operational Risk, Project IRR, Reputation Risk

Solution: Risks: The cost of setting up the capability may be materially higher than anticipated [½]
And/or benefits may not materialise [½]
Leading to the project IRR not being delivered. [½]
It may be difficult to find the necessary skills to assess claims and provide treatment. [½]
These skills may not be available in the market [½]
or it may be much more expensive to employ these individuals than the salaries the company anticipated. [½]
There may be reputational risks involved in recommending / actively managing treatment options for claims in payment. [½]
Team does not operate in a way that meets regulatory requirements. [½]

(v).question: Suggest possible actions Company X could take to mitigate the risks identified in part (iv).
(v).synopsis: Risk Mitigation, Outsourcing, pilots, medical networks

Solution: Cost/benefits differing from expectations could be mitigated by piloting or outsourcing the capability to test assumptions on costs and benefits [½]
Or by reducing model risk through checking data points with reinsurers/consultants. [½]
Existing claims assessment staff could be retrained [½]
Or the company could enter into network agreements with doctor or hospital groups. [½]
The capability could be introduced conservatively and only applied to cases with a clear path to recovery. [½]
Terms and conditions should be clear and sales processes should be updated to ensure that customers are aware that the Company may require treatment. [½]
